div.head: APPENDIX A PROOF OF THEOREM 1
div.p: We provide the proof details of our main result, presented in Theorem 1. The main procedure follows the seminar work [25] and we note that the challenge lies in Lemma. 2 which we believe is new and different from results in [39], [44].

div.head: A.1 Preliminaries and Existing Results
div.p: For the sake of completeness, we present the definitions and existing results used in the proof.

div.head: APPENDIX B IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS
div.p: In this section, we present the details of our implementation in order for reproducibility. All experiments are conducted on the machines with Xeon 3175X CPU, 128G memory and RTX8000 GPU with 48 GB memory. The configurations and packages are listed below: • Use maximum sequence length to 30 with the masked probability 0.2. • Two-layer Attention-Intensity-Attention with two heads. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score.

div.head: B.1.2 Dynamic Node Classification
div.p: • Randomly mask graph nodes with probability 0.2.
• Two-layer GATConv and one-layer Attention-Intensity-Attention block with two heads. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for multi-class prediction.

div.head: B.1.3 Traffic Forecasting
div.p: • Randomly mask graph nodes with probability 0.2.
• Two-layer SAGEConv and one-layer Attention-Intensity-Attention with eight heads. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for prediction.

div.head: B.2 Baseline Architectures for Dynamic Link Prediction
div.p: As mentioned, we follow IDCF [72] to build typical GNN architectures. Here we introduce the details for them.
GAT. We use the GATConv layer available in DGL for implementation. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of one-layer GATConv with four heads.
• Add self-loop and use batch normalization for graph convolution in each layer. • Use tanh as the activation.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. GraphSAGE. We use the SAGEConv layer available in DGL for implementation. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of two-layer SAGEConv.
• Add self-loop and use batch normalization for graph convolution in each layer. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. GCN. We use the SGConv layer available in DGL for implementation. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• One-layer SGConv with two hops.
• Add self-loop and use batch normalization for graph convolution in each layer. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score.
ChebyNet. We use the ChebConv layer available in DGL for implementation. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• One-layer ChebConv with two hops.
• Add self-loop and use batch normalization for graph convolution in each layer. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. ARMA. We use the ARMAConv layer available in DGL for implementation. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• One-layer ARMAConv with two hops.
• Add self-loop and use batch normalization for graph convolution in each layer. • Use tanh as the activation.
• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score.
We also summarize the implementation details of the compared sequential and temporal baselines as follows.
GRU4REC. 9 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of two GRU cells.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. SASREC. 10 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of two-block Transformer with four heads on Koubei, eight heads on Tmall and eight head on Netflix.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. GREC. 11 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:

• A sequence of six-layer dilated CNN with degree 1, 2, 2, 4, 4, 8. • Use maximum sequence length to 30 with the masked probability 0.2. • Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. S2PNM. 12 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of one-block GRU-Transformer.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. BERT4REC. 13 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of three-block Transformer with eight heads.

• Use maximum sequence length to 30 with the masked probability 0.2. • Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. DyREP. 14 We use the software provided by the third party for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of one Attention-RNN Layer.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use linear layer of user embedding and item embedding with softplus activation as ranking score. TGAT. 15 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of three-block Transformer with one head, time sinusoidal embeddings. • Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. TiSASREC. 16 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of two-block Transformer with eight heads with time embedding. • Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. TGREC. 17 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of three-block Transformer with time sinusoidal embeddings. • Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. TimelyREC. 18 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of one-block Attention-Attention.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. CTSMA. 19 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of two-block Transformer with four heads.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. TiCoSeREC. 20 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below: • Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. PTGCN. 21 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• A sequence of two-block Transformer with four heads.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.

• Use inner product between user embedding and item embedding as ranking score. NeuFilter. 22 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Two GRU cells separately for item and user sequence.
• Use maximum sequence length to 30.
• Concatenate user embeddings and item embeddings as the input of an multi-layer network which outputs ranking score.

div.head: B.3 Baseline Architectures for Dynamic Node Classification
div.p: The configurations for static graph models are identical to the dynamic link prediction task except the decoder module that is replaced by one-layer Linear.
In the following, we present the architectures for new dynamic graph models.
DySAT. 23 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past five graph snapshots as input.
• A sequence of three-layer GATConv and one-layer Tem-poralAttention with two heads. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for multi-class prediction. EvolveGCN. 24 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past five graph snapshots as input.
• One-layer gated GRUCell to update the parameters of two-layer GCNConv. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for multi-class prediction. JODIE. 25 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past five graph snapshots as input.
• One-layer gated GRUCell to update the hidden states read out from two-layer TemporalTransformer. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for multi-class prediction. TGN. 26 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past five graph snapshots as input with sinusoidal time embedding. • Three-layer TemporalTransformer with five heads.
• One-layer time-decayed Recurrent unit to sequentially update node embeddings over time. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for multi-class prediction. RoLAND. 27 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:

• Two-layer node embedding updation modules. In each layer, we first adopt the vanilla message-passing to obtain preliminary node embeddings. Then, these preliminary node embeddings and the node embeddings output from the previous layer are both input into a one-layer GRU to obtain the final node embedding for this layer. • Use PReLU as the activation.
• Use a MLP implemented by two-layer Linear for multiclass prediction. DEFT. 28 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past five graph snapshots as input.
• Two-layer ChebConv with extra spectral filtering.
• Use RReLU as the activation.
• Use a MLP implemented by two-layer Linear for multiclass prediction. SpikeNet. 29 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use all past graph snapshots as input.
• Wwo-layer GraphSage with temporal neighborhood sampler on graphs. • Use LIF (Leaky integrate-and-fire) as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for multi-class prediction.

div.head: B.4 Baseline Architectures for Traffic Forecasting
div.p: The configurations for static graph models are identical to the dynamic link prediction task except the decoder module that is replaced by one-layer Linear.
In the following, we present the architectures for new dynamic graph models.
DCRNN. We use the software provided in DGL library. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.
• One-layer ChebConv with two hops.
• One-layer GraphRNN to sequential update the node embeddings over time. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for prediction. GaAN. We use the software provided in DGL library. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.

• One-layer gated graph attention that considers the edge weights. • Use one-layer Linear for prediction. STGCN. We use the software provided in DGL library. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.
• A sequence of TNTSTNTST, where T, N, S represents temporal convolutional neural network, LayerNorm and spatial graph convolutional neural network, respectively.
• Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for prediction. DSTAGNN. 30 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.
• Four-layer DSTAGNN with four heads each of which uses ChebConv with three hops. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use Conv2d and Linear for prediction. Trafformer. 31 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.
• Two-layer Transformer-style encoder with two heads.
• One-layer Transformer-style decoder with two heads.
• Use GeLU as the activation.
• Use one-layer Linear for prediction. TrendGCN. 32 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.

• Two-layer Linear discriminator with LeakyReLU which focuses on the trend of individual time series. • Two-layer Linear discriminator with LeakyReLU which emphasizes the correlation of multivariate time series. • Two-layer ChebConv and two-layer GRU.
• Use one-layer Linear for prediction. STID 33 We use the software provided by the authors for experiments. The detailed architecture description is as below:
• Use past twelve graph snapshots as input.
• proposes aa framework with a embedding layer (by Conv2d), multiple MLP layers, and a regression layer. • Use ReLU as the activation.
• Use Linear for prediction.

div.head: B.5 Choice of Hyper-parameters
div.p: Regarding the choice of optimizer, we use Adam [36] if not specified and the number of epochs is 200. We search by grid the embedding size ranging in {64, 128, . . . , 512}, learning rate {1e-5, 1e-4, . . . , 1e-1}, dropout rate {0.1, 0.2, . . . , 0.7}, batch size {64, 128, . . . , 512} and ℓ 2 regularizer {1e-5, 1e-

div.head: APPENDIX C DATASET PROCESSING
div.p: We present more dataset details in this section.

div.head: C.1 Time Scaling
div.p: We use one hour, one day and one week to scale the time data on the Netflix, Tmall and Koubei datasets, respectively. The choice of time unit is determined by the averaged time between two consecutive events for each user. We warn that again the time is discrete on the Elliptic data and the traffic speed readings on META-LA are record every five minutes. For both of these two datasets, we apply no modifications to the time data.

div.head: C.2 Random Seed
div.p: We use five random seeds to yield different data splits, i.e., 12345, 54321, 56789, 98765 and 7401.

div.head: C.3 Normalization on the META-LA data
div.p: We calculate the mean and the standard deviation of the training readings on each road (node). When making predictions, we use these quantities to scale down the input readings and scale up the output readings. We found by experiments that this treatment can significantly reduce the RMSE and MAPE errors.

We also study the influence of the neighborhood hops ranging from 1 to 3, the number of graph filtering blocks from 1 up to 4 and the number of heads in {1, 2, . . . , 8}. We warn that we set the embedding size to 32 for fair comparisons when evaluating the performance on the Ellicit and META-LA datasets. This is because TGN, DCRNN and DSTAGNN take days to complete the training if embedding size is greater than 64
